skill.name: @azure/cosmos (TypeScript/JavaScript)
skill.description: Azure Cosmos DB JavaScript/TypeScript SDK for data plane operations including CRUD on documents, queries, and bulk operations.
skill.summary: Data plane SDK for Azure Cosmos DB NoSQL API operations with TypeScript support.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Prompt-only instructional skill containing documentation for Azure Cosmos DB SDK usage. Static analysis scanned 0 files with 0 findings and risk score 0/100. No executable code, network access, or security concerns detected. The skill teaches proper security practices including AAD authentication and parameterized queries.

content.user_title: Build Cosmos DB applications with TypeScript
content.value_statement: Developers need to perform CRUD operations on Azure Cosmos DB documents efficiently. This skill provides TypeScript patterns for queries, bulk operations, and proper authentication.
actual_capabilities: Create, read, update, and delete documents in Cosmos DB containers
Execute parameterized SQL queries with cross-partition support
Perform bulk operations for efficient batch processing
Implement optimistic concurrency control with ETags
Configure hierarchical partition keys for multi-level data organization
Handle Cosmos DB errors with proper retry logic
limitations: Only supports Cosmos DB NoSQL API (not MongoDB, Cassandra, or Gremlin APIs)
Requires Azure Cosmos DB account and proper authentication credentials
Does not manage Cosmos DB resources (use @azure/arm-cosmosdb for management plane)
Network access to Cosmos DB endpoint required for all operations

use_cases.title: Backend developer building a SaaS application
use_cases.description: Implement a multi-tenant data layer using hierarchical partition keys to isolate customer data while maintaining query performance.

use_cases.title: Data engineer processing high-volume transactions
use_cases.description: Use bulk operations to efficiently insert, update, and delete thousands of documents per second with proper error handling.

use_cases.title: Full-stack developer creating real-time applications
use_cases.description: Build change feed processors and implement optimistic concurrency to handle concurrent updates from multiple clients.

prompt_templates.title: Basic document creation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TypeScript function that inserts a new product document into Azure Cosmos DB. The product should have id, name, price, and category fields. Use DefaultAzureCredential for authentication and include proper error handling for duplicate documents.

prompt_templates.title: Parameterized query implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a TypeScript service method that queries Cosmos DB for products within a price range. Use SqlQuerySpec with named parameters to prevent injection attacks. Include pagination support with continuation tokens.

prompt_templates.title: Bulk operation for data migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a bulk import function that processes an array of 1000+ documents using executeBulkOperations. Handle partial failures by logging failed operations with their status codes while continuing successful ones.

prompt_templates.title: Optimistic concurrency with ETags
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an update function that uses ETags to prevent lost updates when multiple users modify the same document. Return a specific error when the ETag precondition fails so the client can refresh and retry.

output_examples.input: Create a product document with name 'Laptop', price 999.99, category 'electronics'
output_examples.output: Successfully created document with id 'prod-8a7b2c' in container 'products'. Request charge: 5.21 RUs.

output_examples.input: Query products where price < 1000 and category = 'electronics'
output_examples.output: Retrieved 15 products matching criteria. Total request charge: 3.47 RUs. Results include: Laptop, Tablet, Wireless Mouse, USB Hub...

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential for authentication instead of account keys in production environments
Always use parameterized queries with SqlQuerySpec to prevent injection and improve query plan caching
Specify partition key values in all point operations to avoid cross-partition query overhead
anti_patterns: Using account keys directly in code instead of environment variables or managed identity
Executing SELECT * queries without WHERE clauses on large containers
Ignoring 429 rate limit errors instead of implementing exponential backoff retry logic

faq.question: What authentication method should I use for Cosmos DB?
faq.answer: Use DefaultAzureCredential with Azure AD authentication for production. It supports managed identity, CLI credentials, and environment variables. Only use account keys for local development.

faq.question: How do I choose a good partition key?
faq.answer: Select a property with high cardinality and even distribution. Common choices include customerId, tenantId, or a synthetic key. Avoid properties with low cardinality like status or boolean values.

faq.question: What is the difference between create, upsert, and replace?
faq.answer: Create fails if the document exists. Upsert creates or updates based on id. Replace requires the document to exist and replaces the entire document. Use patch for partial updates.

faq.question: How do I handle rate limiting (429 errors)?
faq.answer: The SDK includes built-in retry logic. For custom handling, catch ErrorResponse with code 429 and use the retryAfterInMs property to implement exponential backoff.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with Cosmos DB MongoDB API?
faq.answer: No. This skill is for the NoSQL (SQL) API only. For MongoDB API, use the official MongoDB drivers or the Azure Cosmos DB MongoDB driver.

faq.question: How do I optimize query performance?
faq.answer: Use selective projections instead of SELECT *, filter with WHERE clauses on indexed properties, specify partitionKey in FeedOptions, and enable cross-partition queries only when necessary.
